narrative_summary: The analysis identifies Oneil Cruz as the leader in average bat speed at 78.79 mph, followed closely by Junior Caminero at 78.54 mph. A clear correlation exists between elite bat speed and 'Hard Swing Rate,' with Caminero reaching a peak of 81.1%. However, the data reveals a high-variance performance profile among these power hitters; while bat speed is exceptional, players like Oneil Cruz and Jordan Walker exhibit significant negative 'Batter Run Values' (-21.06 and -19.14 respectively), often paired with higher 'Whiff per Swing' rates (32.3%). This suggests that while bat speed provides the raw physical ceiling for production, it must be balanced with contact quality to translate into positive run value.
python_code:
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# Load dataset (assuming data is in a csv named 'swing_data.csv')
df = pd.read_csv('swing_data.csv')

# Filter and sort for Top 10 by average bat speed
top_10_speed = df.sort_values(by='avg_bat_speed', ascending=False).head(10)

# Set visualization style
sns.set_theme(style='whitegrid')
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))

# Create Barplot
plot = sns.barplot(data=top_10_speed, x='avg_bat_speed', y='name', palette='magma')
plt.title('Top 10 MLB Players by Average Bat Speed', fontsize=15)
plt.xlabel('Average Bat Speed (mph)', fontsize=12)
plt.ylabel('Player Name', fontsize=12)
plt.xlim(70, 80)

# Adding annotations
for i, v in enumerate(top_10_speed['avg_bat_speed']):
    plot.text(v + 0.1, i, f'{v:.2f}', color='black', va='center')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

notebook_cells.content:
# Analysis of Elite Bat Speed Leaders
This notebook explores the relationship between high-velocity swings and overall hitting efficiency using the latest Statcast swing metrics.

import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Initialize Data
data = [
    {'name': 'Cruz, Oneil', 'avg_bat_speed': 78.79, 'hard_swing_rate': 0.78, 'batter_run_value': -21.06},
    {'name': 'Caminero, Junior', 'avg_bat_speed': 78.54, 'hard_swing_rate': 0.81, 'batter_run_value': 2.37},
    {'name': 'Walker, Jordan', 'avg_bat_speed': 78.07, 'hard_swing_rate': 0.77, 'batter_run_value': -19.14},
    {'name': 'Adell, Jo', 'avg_bat_speed': 77.61, 'hard_swing_rate': 0.73, 'batter_run_value': 0.66},
    {'name': 'Schwarber, Kyle', 'avg_bat_speed': 77.32, 'hard_swing_rate': 0.77, 'batter_run_value': 11.19}
]
df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Sorting for analysis
df_sorted = df.sort_values('avg_bat_speed', ascending=False)
df_sorted

## Visualization: Bat Speed vs. Run Value
While high speed is desirable, we evaluate if it translates directly to run production.

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))
sns.scatterplot(data=df, x='avg_bat_speed', y='batter_run_value', size='hard_swing_rate', hue='name', sizes=(100, 400))
plt.title('Bat Speed vs. Batter Run Value')
plt.legend(bbox_to_anchor=(1.05, 1), loc=2)
plt.show()
